justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance solved via Redis Lua compare-and-swap — functionally equivalent to SET NX but stronger, with explicit Lua script provided and explanation of why single-threaded execution guarantees mutual exclusion. (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with durability trade-off explicitly acknowledged in trade-offs table ('Data loss on Redis crash loses in-flight bid windows'). (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed via dedicated Timer Service polling a sorted set, triggering BidWindowExpired event to Assignment Service which queries highest-rated available courier. (4) Hot zone/key concentration risk identified ('500 orders all in one zone overwhelming 30 couriers') with mitigation. (5) Durability vs. latency trade-off explicitly enumerated — Redis sub-ms atomicity gained, data loss on crash sacrificed.

observations.text: Lua script provided verbatim with clear correctness argument — goes beyond SET NX to a full state-machine transition, which is architecturally stronger
Explicitly rejects database-first approach: 'Redis Lua is 10-100× faster than SELECT ... FOR UPDATE in Postgres'
Timer reliability addressed with sorted set polling instead of fragile keyspace notifications, with dual-instance ZPOPMIN for exactly-once processing
Clock skew risk identified and mitigated by using Redis TIME as single clock source
Idempotent accept handling designed — courier retry after network loss returns correct result
Recovery path for Redis failure: re-emit BidWindowOpened for unassigned orders after 30 seconds

Hot key risk framed as a zone-level business problem rather than a Redis-shard-level data distribution problem — though mitigated by the single-instance decision, this would become critical if the system scales to Redis Cluster
Rigidity points are well-chosen — correctly identifies the Lua script as the single authority for claim decisions and warns against any bypass

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

The courier bidding system is a **time-bounded, first-writer-wins concurrency problem** at its core. The architecture centers on Redis as the atomic state arbiter for bid windows, WebSocket fan-out for real-time courier notification, and a timer-based expiry mechanism for auto-assignment fallback. At 500 concurrent bid windows × 30 couriers, the system is well within single-Redis-instance capacity, so the design prioritizes simplicity and correctness over distributed comple
